metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` block (or use a `data`-source-only reference pattern) on the `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` SSM resource, and call this out in the plan as the protection against key loss on re-apply.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is described as 'declared; value seeded out-of-band,' but the plan specifies no `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` guard or equivalent protection. On any subsequent `terraform apply`, Terraform will attempt to reconcile the parameter value back to whatever the resource declaration specifies — silently overwriting or nulling the seeded private key, which 'exists nowhere else.' This is an irreversible key loss with no rollback path.
findings.location: What section — Terraform subsection, SSM parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; Acceptance Criteria bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform resource for the key parameter, add `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` so Terraform tracks the resource's existence but never overwrites the externally-seeded secret value. Document this as the explicit, justified choice for out-of-band secret seeding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' description to specify the precise HTTP API mechanism: `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` set on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` (not a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource), and add an AC assertion that ALL routes on the API carry this setting.
findings.finding: The plan describes 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' using REST API (v1) terminology. For an API Gateway HTTP API (v2 / APIGatewayV2), AWS_IAM authentication is not implemented with a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource — HTTP API authorizers support only `JWT` and `REQUEST` types. AWS_IAM for HTTP API is instead configured directly on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` with no authorizer resource. An implementer following the plan's 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' framing who creates an `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` with `authorizer_type = "AWS_IAM"` will receive a Terraform/provider error; alternatively, an implementer who creates an authorizer at API level but omits `authorization_type` on individual routes may silently leave routes open to unsigned requests.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with: 'each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` carries `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` (the correct HTTP API v2 mechanism — there is no separate aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer resource for AWS_IAM on HTTP APIs)'; add an AC line asserting that a test verifies authorization_type = AWS_IAM on every declared route.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the What section that HTTP API AWS_IAM auth is set via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` (not via a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource), and confirm every route is covered.
findings.finding: The plan uses the phrase 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' — language that maps to REST API v1 semantics, where a named `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource with `authorizer_type = "AWS_IAM"` could be expected. In API Gateway HTTP API (v2), AWS_IAM auth is set per-route via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on `aws_apigatewayv2_route`; there is no separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource for AWS_IAM — only JWT and REQUEST/LAMBDA authorizer types exist for HTTP APIs.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with 'AWS_IAM authorization set per-route (`authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route`)' to prevent the v1 authorizer-resource confusion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` on both new SSM SecureString parameters (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` and `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`) in the plan's IaC declarations, following the pattern already established in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` on the two new SSM SecureString parameters (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`). Without it, a subsequent `terraform apply` would overwrite the operator-seeded Ed25519 private key and the materialized origin-guard with Terraform's placeholder value, breaking the deployed service.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString declarations; AC line 1 ('the two SecureString parameters … under the existing rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read path grant')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to both new `aws_ssm_parameter` blocks: `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` — identical to the existing `aws_ssm_parameter.rebar_secrets` resource in ssm.tf. Document in the AC that this block is required so the test assertion can verify it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each 'a test asserts' AC (1, 3, 4), state the concrete test file path, framework, and invocation command (e.g. `pytest infra/tests/test_opcert_iac.py::test_api_gw_declarations -v`) so a reviewer has a deterministic proving command.
findings.finding: ACs 1, 3, and 4 each say 'a test asserts …' without naming the test file, runner, or invocation command. An executor knows WHAT to assert but has no concrete proving command to run in-session to produce evidence of passage — violating E6's requirement that every completion-relevant claim has a concrete proving command that produces evidence on success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('A test asserts these declarations exist'), AC3 ('a test asserts the service entry…'), AC4 ('a test asserts the entry parses…')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single line to each AC naming the test file and runner: e.g. AC1: 'Verified by `pytest infra/tests/test_opcert_iac.py -v` (covers the three sub-assertions)'; similarly for AC3 (`pytest infra/tests/test_opcert_compose.py -v`) and AC4 (`pytest infra/tests/test_trusted_envs.py -v`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with the precise HTTP API mechanism: `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` (no separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource is used — HTTP APIs do not support an AWS_IAM authorizer type), and confirm the AC and IaC notes reflect this route-level configuration.
findings.finding: The plan uses the phrase 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' for the API Gateway HTTP API, which echoes the REST API v1 concept of a named `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource. For HTTP APIs (APIGatewayV2), AWS_IAM auth is NOT a named authorizer resource — `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` only accepts `authorizer_type = "JWT"` or `"REQUEST"` (Lambda) for HTTP APIs. AWS_IAM is configured via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` set on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` individually. An implementer following the plan's 'authorizer' framing would create an `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` with `authorizer_type = "AWS_IAM"`, which Terraform will reject at validate/apply time; or they would apply AWS_IAM at the API level rather than per-route, leaving routes open.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer, invoke permission over TLS'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' to 'an API Gateway HTTP API (APIGatewayV2) with `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on every `aws_apigatewayv2_route` for the `/opcert/` paths — no separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource (HTTP APIs only support JWT and Lambda REQUEST authorizer types; AWS_IAM is route-level only).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare the backend integration URI scheme in the plan (and the corresponding AC): specify that the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` backend URI is `https://` (not `http://`), or explain how the guard header's confidentiality is preserved on the API GW → origin hop when VPC Link is absent and port 80 is open to the internet.
findings.finding: The plan's backend integration leg — API Gateway → EC2 nginx — is described as 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' with VPC Link explicitly rejected, meaning the traffic traverses the public internet. The phrase 'over TLS' in the same sentence qualifies only the client-facing invocation path, not the backend leg. If the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` backend URI is `http://` (the box's public IP on port 80 or the origin DNS name on port 80), the `X-Opcert-Guard` header secret and certificate response bodies travel in cleartext over the public internet after API Gateway strips SigV4 — a passive network observer can extract the guard value and use it to hit nginx port 80 directly without going through API Gateway, defeating the inner perimeter entirely. Port 80 is confirmed open to `0.0.0.0/0` in network.tf for ACME.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer, invoke permission over TLS, HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the `/opcert/` routes, injecting a shared origin-guard header'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the API Gateway HTTP API backend integration URI MUST use https:// (the box's nginx TLS endpoint on port 443) rather than http://, and add this as an explicit constraint in the What section and AC1.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that API Gateway is 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' with VPC Link explicitly rejected — meaning the integration traverses the public internet. The 'over TLS' qualifier in the sentence 'invoke permission over TLS' refers to the client-facing invocation path, not the backend integration leg. The plan never specifies the backend integration URI scheme (http:// vs https://). If the integration URI is http://, the X-Opcert-Guard secret value (generated by `random_password` and mapped as a static header) and all service responses traverse the public internet in cleartext after API Gateway strips SigV4 — undermining the confidentiality of the guard secret and the integrity of the payload. Port 80 is open on the EC2 box (for ACME challenges), making http:// backend reachable.
findings.location: What section — 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the /opcert/ routes, injecting a shared origin-guard header'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' to 'HTTPS-integrated to the box's nginx TLS origin (https://<box-dns>:443/opcert/) — the backend integration URI scheme must be https:// so the X-Opcert-Guard header value is never transmitted in cleartext over the public internet.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the API Gateway → EC2 origin integration URI scheme explicitly: either (a) specify that the integration URI is `https://` (using the existing Let's Encrypt certificate on the box) and assert this in an AC, or (b) if http:// is intentional, document the threat-model rationale and add a compensating control (e.g., restricting port 80 on the SG to APIGW IP ranges or a private subnet).
findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 origin integration protocol is undeclared: the plan says 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' without specifying whether the backend URI scheme is http:// or https://. VPC Link is explicitly rejected, so the hop crosses the public internet (default VPC, all subnets public). If the integration URI is http://, the X-Opcert-Guard shared secret and all certificate-material responses traverse the public internet in cleartext after API Gateway strips SigV4, exposing the guard value to passive interception and enabling direct origin bypass.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API … invoke permission over TLS, HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section change 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' to 'HTTPS-integrated to the box's nginx origin (using the existing Let's Encrypt certificate; integration URI `https://<dns-name>/opcert/`)'. Add to AC1: 'the integration URI uses the https:// scheme (origin leg is TLS-protected)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and explicitly cite the exact `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` key format (e.g., `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) required to inject a static header in an HTTP API integration, and add the verified key to the AC or a code reference.
findings.finding: The plan asserts Terraform can inject the origin-guard value as a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, but does not cite or verify the exact `request_parameters` key format required. The HTTP API integration `request_parameters` attribute uses a non-obvious key syntax (e.g., `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` or `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) distinct from REST API (v1) integration parameter mapping; an incorrect key is silently accepted by `terraform apply` while never injecting the header at runtime.
findings.location: What section — 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit deploy-order sequence with rollback steps for each stage: (1) run `nginx -t` to validate config before reload; (2) if compose deploy fails after `terraform apply`, specify the recovery action (e.g., disable the API Gateway stage or run `terraform apply` with the API Gateway removed); and document these in the Deploy + prove section or a linked runbook.
findings.finding: The deploy ordering between `terraform apply` and the compose + materialize steps on the box is implied but not explicitly sequenced, and no rollback path is defined for partial failures (e.g., Terraform succeeds but materialize/compose fails, leaving API Gateway live with no backend). An operator following the plan could leave API Gateway routing to a non-running service with no documented recovery steps.
findings.location: What / Deploy + prove section; AC bullet 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [result] }` (or equivalent) to the `random_password` resource so it is never silently replaced on re-apply, OR specify the operational procedure that must follow any guard rotation (re-run materialize + nginx reload) and add a Terraform output or CI check that detects guard-value drift between SSM and the running nginx conf.
findings.finding: If `terraform apply` is re-run and the `random_password` resource is replaced (e.g., state taint, state loss, or provider upgrade), Terraform updates the API Gateway static header to the new guard value, but the nginx conf on the box still holds the old value until `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is manually re-run. The plan specifies no re-materialize trigger or guard against this divergence, so all opcert calls will silently 401/403 until an operator notices and re-materializes.
findings.location: What section — origin-guard provisioning path; `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`; AC bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [result] }` to the `random_password.opcert_guard` resource in Terraform so the guard value is stable across re-applies. Document that intentional rotation requires: (1) `terraform taint random_password.opcert_guard`, (2) `terraform apply`, (3) immediate re-run of `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` on the box, (4) `nginx -s reload`. Optionally add a Terraform output `opcert_guard_ssm_version` so a health-check script can detect drift.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `hashicorp/random` provider addition is justified over the established `CHANGEME`+`ignore_changes`+operator-seed pattern (used for all 14 existing SSM secrets) — if the guard value can be generated locally by the operator (e.g., `openssl rand -hex 32`) and seeded like other secrets, remove the new provider dependency.
findings.finding: The `hashicorp/random` provider is introduced as a new provider dependency with exactly one use-site (`random_password` for the origin-guard value), while the codebase's established pattern for every other secret (14 existing SSM params) is the `CHANGEME` placeholder + `ignore_changes` + operator-seeds-out-of-band pattern — which already handles this class of need without a new provider.
findings.location: What section: 'the hashicorp/random provider is ADDED to infra/terraform/versions.tf required_providers'; infra/terraform/versions.tf:21-26; infra/terraform/ssm.tf:60-70
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `hashicorp/random`; add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to the existing `rebar_secret_params` list in `ssm.tf` (CHANGEME placeholder + ignore_changes, same as all other secrets). The materialize script reads it from SSM regardless of how it was seeded. Document the one-time operator step: `openssl rand -hex 32 | aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --type SecureString --value $(cat) --overwrite`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step/AC item requiring that the updated `infra/terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl` (containing the `hashicorp/random` provider hash) is committed as part of this story's diff.
findings.finding: The plan states 'terraform init refreshes `.terraform.lock.hcl`' after adding `hashicorp/random` to `required_providers`, but the current `.terraform.lock.hcl` contains only the `hashicorp/aws` provider entry (verified by reading the file). Adding a new provider via `terraform init` mutates the lockfile — this updated `.terraform.lock.hcl` (with the new hashicorp/random hash entries) must be committed as part of the story's deliverables, but the plan does not include committing it as a step or AC item. The AC for `terraform fmt -check` and `terraform validate` passing does not cover this.
findings.location: What section: Terraform bullet — 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to infra/terraform/versions.tf `required_providers` … `terraform init` refreshes `.terraform.lock.hcl`'; AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Deploy steps: 'run `terraform init` after adding the provider, then commit the updated `.terraform.lock.hcl`'; add a CI/AC note that the lockfile must be up-to-date.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` as an explicit edit target: wire the new `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` call into compose-up.sh (before `docker compose up`) following the existing `materialize-g2p-config.sh` pattern (lines 172-178).
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is an unenumerated consumer: every existing materialize script (materialize-deploy-key.sh, materialize-g2p-config.sh) is called from compose-up.sh before `docker compose up` (lines 172-178 of compose-up.sh), but the plan does not list compose-up.sh as an edit target despite specifying a new `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` that follows the same precedent and must be wired into the same boot sequence.
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh
findings.suggested_fix: In compose-up.sh, add a block analogous to the g2p materialization (lines 172-178): `if ! bash "${REPO_ROOT}/infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh"; then echo "compose-up: WARN — opcert guard materialization failed" >&2; fi` — placing it before the `docker compose up` call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` as an explicit edit target: either add the `/opcert/` guarded location block directly to the template, or add an `include /etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf;` directive to the HTTPS server block and document that the materialize script writes to that include path.
findings.finding: `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` is an unenumerated edit target: the plan specifies a 'nginx route `/opcert/` → the service that rejects requests whose `X-Opcert-Guard` header does not match', and describes this as a 'nginx conf include', but the existing nginx configuration is a single template file (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`) with no include mechanism — adding the `/opcert/` guarded route requires modifying this template or adding an include directive to it, yet neither the template file nor any mechanism to add an include is listed as an edit target.
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring; infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as an edit target (or explicitly acknowledge it as out-of-scope for this story): either extend `BOT_PATHS` to include `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` so the opcert service auto-redeploys on Dockerfile changes, or document why the opcert service is intentionally excluded from the auto-deploy surface.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh`'s `BOT_PATHS` variable is an unenumerated consumer: it currently lists `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` but NOT `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert`, so changes to the opcert Dockerfile will not trigger an auto-redeploy of the opcert service via the continuous deploy loop.
findings.location: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh line 55; What section — Deploy + prove
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh line 55, change `BOT_PATHS` to: `BOT_PATHS='src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'` — and add a corresponding opcert service rebuild step analogous to the review-bot rebuild, or note if the opcert service is intentionally operator-only managed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [result] }` to the `random_password` resource so it is never silently replaced on re-apply, OR document the mandatory post-apply step (re-run materialize-opcert-guard.sh + nginx reload) that must follow any guard rotation as an explicit AC requirement with a Terraform output that surfaces the guard SSM parameter name as the operator's cue.
findings.finding: The plan uses `random_password` to generate the origin-guard value and map it into API Gateway as a static header, but specifies no `lifecycle { ignore_changes }` on the resource and no re-materialization trigger. If Terraform replaces the `random_password` resource on any re-apply (state taint, state loss, provider upgrade), API Gateway is updated to the new guard value while nginx on the box still holds the old materialized value — silently breaking every opcert request with no deploy-time signal and no alert. This is a hidden ordering/atomicity assumption: two systems (API GW and nginx) must hold the same guard value, but only one is updated atomically by Terraform.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`… stores it as SSM SecureString… maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [result] }` to the `random_password.opcert_origin_guard` resource declaration; expose the guard's SSM parameter name as a Terraform output and note in the deploy procedure that nginx must be re-materialized if that output ever changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider replacing `random_password` + `hashicorp/random` with the established SSM operator-seed + `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` pattern (following the monitoring.tf precedent where `data.aws_ssm_parameter.alert_endpoint.value` is injected directly into a Terraform resource), which achieves the same runtime result with zero new providers and eliminates the rotation-idempotency gap; if `random_password` is retained, provide a positive rationale for why Terraform-managed randomness is preferred over operator-seeded consistency with the existing 12-secret pattern.
findings.finding: The plan introduces the `hashicorp/random` provider and `random_password` for the origin-guard value — a single use-site — when the codebase's established pattern (ssm.tf, 12 entries) is CHANGEME placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` + operator CLI seeding, and monitoring.tf already demonstrates the exact dual-use complement: `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` reads an operator-seeded SecureString and injects its value directly into a Terraform resource (SNS subscription endpoint). Applying that same pattern here — operator seeds the origin-guard via `aws ssm put-parameter`, Terraform reads it with `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` and maps it into the API Gateway integration — achieves the identical runtime result with zero new provider dependencies, zero rotation-idempotency risk, and full consistency with all existing precedents. The plan provides no positive rationale for why `random_password` is preferred over this established approach.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`… the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` in `ssm.tf` alongside the other CHANGEME secrets (lifecycle ignore_changes). Add `data "aws_ssm_parameter" "opcert_origin_guard" { name = "/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard"; with_decryption = true }` and use `data.aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_origin_guard.value` as the static header value in the integration — the same shape monitoring.tf uses for the alert endpoint. Remove the hashicorp/random provider addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC4's `trusted_environments.yaml` pinning instruction to use `added_at_log_position` (the tickets-branch log position, operator-stamped via `rebar trusted-env`) instead of `added_at_commit` (the superseded main-SHA field), per the parent epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' Option B resolution.
findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 pins the deployed environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with the field `added_at_commit` (a main-branch git SHA), but the parent epic's authoritative 'Authoritative era anchor' resolution (security finding #5) superseded the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section and replaced `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` with `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions). The leaf's pinning instruction references the superseded schema, creating a direct contradiction with the parent's current authoritative field names.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 / `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` pinning step
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 parenthetical `(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)` with `(with `added_at_log_position` set to the current tickets-branch tip via `rebar trusted-env`, per the epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' Option B protocol)`. Also update the Deploy + prove step in 'What' to include running `rebar trusted-env` to stamp the correct log position at pinning time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `hashicorp/random` + `random_password` is preferred over the project's established operator-seeded SSM pattern (`CHANGEME` placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`) for the origin-guard value, or replace it with the established pattern to avoid a new provider dependency.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) as a new provider solely to generate the origin-guard value, while the codebase's established pattern for all 12 SSM secrets is CHANGEME placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` + operator CLI seeding (ssm.tf `rebar_secret_params`). The plan provides no justification for why `random_password` is preferred over the existing operator-seeded pattern at this one call site.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`… the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `hashicorp/random` from `required_providers`; add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to `ssm.tf`'s `rebar_secret_params` list (or as a standalone `aws_ssm_parameter` with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`); add a `data "aws_ssm_parameter" "opcert_origin_guard"` block; reference `.value` in the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` map. This matches monitoring.tf's `alert_endpoint` precedent exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address random_password state-loss / recreation risk in the plan: either document the re-materialization runbook step required after any Terraform recreate of the origin-guard, or add `keepers` to the `random_password` resource pinned to a stable input (e.g., account ID or a version counter) so accidental recreation is blocked.
findings.finding: The origin-guard value is generated by `random_password` and stored in Terraform state and SSM, but the plan does not address what happens if Terraform state is lost or the resource is recreated: a new random value would be generated, updating SSM and the API Gateway integration header atomically, but leaving the on-box nginx conf stale until `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is manually re-run — creating a split-brain where the API GW injects the new header value that nginx rejects. No recovery procedure or `keepers` pinning is described.
findings.location: What section — 'Terraform generates the guard value with random_password … stores it as SSM SecureString … maps it as a static request-header parameter (X-Opcert-Guard) on the API Gateway integration'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the `env_id` generation rule inline (or link to the exact era-protocol section), e.g. 'env_id is a stable lowercase UUID generated once per environment deployment (`uuidgen | tr A-Z a-z`) and recorded as `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` in the compose service env'.
findings.finding: The `env_id` value's format and generation procedure are deferred entirely to the 'epic's era protocol' without stating a default or pointing to a doc that an executor can consult. An executor deploying for the first time cannot determine what value to assign to `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` or record in `trusted_environments.yaml` without resolving an external, unlinked dependency.
findings.location: What section (compose wiring, key pinning steps) and AC4 ('the `env_id` matches the compose service's `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Deploy+prove bullet: 'Generate `env_id` as a random UUID (`uuidgen | tr A-Z a-z`); record it as `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` in the compose service env and in `trusted_environments.yaml`.' Update AC4 to include the format assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the specific file path `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` to Acceptance Criterion bullet 3 so the test has an unambiguous target matching the deliverable named in the 'What' section.
findings.finding: Acceptance Criterion bullet 3 requires a test asserting 'the materialize script (per the `infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh` precedent) renders the guard value from its SSM parameter,' but does not name the specific file the test should target. The 'What' section names it `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`. Without the path in the AC, an implementer writing the test has no canonical contract for which file to assert on — creating a coverage ambiguity between the described deliverable and its verifying criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 3; What section — 'the new script is `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`'
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC bullet 3 to read: '… and that the materialize script `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (per the `infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh` precedent) renders the guard value from its SSM parameter into the nginx conf include and service env.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or reference a formal ADR (e.g., ADR 0048) capturing the SigV4 API Gateway + origin-guard trusted-gate architecture, the rejected alternatives (Fargate/NLB/Secrets Manager), and the zero-fixed-cost constraint — and add an AC line requiring the ADR to be merged alongside the IaC.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — SigV4 API Gateway HTTP API + origin-guard header as the trust enforcement boundary — and references the epic's 'Producer-signing & compute-runtime design resolution' as its design backing, but does not assign or reference a formal ADR number for this specific deployment architecture. The project demonstrably uses numbered ADRs (ADR 0046, ADR 0047 are cited inline). Without a named ADR or design-doc update scoped to this story, the origin-guard pattern and the SigV4 trusted-gate topology have no discoverable permanent record for future operators.
findings.location: Why section ('Deployment decisions are settled in the epic\'s "Producer-signing & compute-runtime design resolution"'); What section (API Gateway + origin-guard architecture)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0048-opcert-trusted-gate-sigv4-origin-guard.md` documenting: (1) the SigV4 HTTP API as the authentication boundary, (2) the origin-guard header as the secondary enforcement layer preventing direct-to-origin bypass, (3) the rejected Fargate/NLB/Secrets-Manager alternative and why, (4) the cost constraint driving the EC2-colocation decision. Reference it in AC line 1 ('A test asserts…') or add a standalone AC: '[ ] ADR 0048 is merged and linked from `infra/terraform/README.md`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE procedure for the two SSM SecureStrings (e.g., `aws ssm delete-parameter` as part of `terraform destroy` or a documented runbook step) and for the trusted_environments.yaml entry (e.g., mark `retired_at_commit`), and note these in the plan's Scope or a linked runbook.
findings.finding: Neither SSM SecureString (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`) nor the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry has a specified RETIRE/cleanup path. If the opcert service is decommissioned, the private key persists in SSM (a live secret leak risk), the origin-guard persists, and the trusted-env entry persists, potentially allowing a retired environment to be trusted indefinitely.
findings.location: What section — SSM parameter declarations; AC bullet 4 (trusted_environments.yaml)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum: (a) a CloudWatch alarm on the API Gateway HTTP API's `5XXError` and `4XXError` metrics, and (b) a non-zero exit / explicit error log in `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` that surfaces to the compose service's stdout (so it appears in `docker compose logs`), and note these in the plan's observability provisions.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (metrics, logs, or alerts) for the new failure points introduced: (1) `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` failures (SSM unavailable, file-write error, partial render), (2) nginx origin-guard rejection rate (unexpected spikes indicate API GW / materialize drift), (3) API Gateway 4xx/5xx error rates for the opcert endpoint. Without instrumentation, operators cannot detect or debug failures.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; materialize script; AC bullet 5 (operator-attested checks)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the placement inconsistency: either move the new script to `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (where the two precedent scripts actually live) and update the plan/AC3 reference, or explicitly justify why `infra/scripts/` is the correct location despite no precedent there.
findings.finding: The plan states the new materialize script follows 'the infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file, same pattern' precedent, but then places the new script at `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`. A grep of `infra/scripts/` shows it contains no `materialize-*.sh` scripts at all (only compose-up.sh, fetch-secrets.sh, autodeploy.sh, etc.); all materialize scripts live under `infra/gerrit/`. The cited precedent location and the specified target location are in different directories with different ownership contexts.
findings.location: What section: 'Compose — extend infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'; AC3: 'the materialize script (per the infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh precedent) renders the guard value from its SSM parameter'
findings.suggested_fix: Place the new script at `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` to match the actual directory where `materialize-deploy-key.sh` and `materialize-g2p-config.sh` live, and update the plan text and AC3 reference accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for the new API Gateway endpoint to the plan: at minimum, an API Gateway access log group and a CloudWatch alarm on 4xx or 5xx error rates (or integration latency), wired to the existing SNS alerts topic, consistent with the monitoring pattern established in `infra/terraform/monitoring*.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new internet-facing API Gateway HTTP API endpoint but specifies no access logging, CloudWatch metrics, or alarms for it. Every other major rebar component (Gerrit, review-bot, autodeploy, disk, host) has a corresponding CloudWatch alarm wired to the SNS alert topic; the opcert API Gateway is the only internet-reachable entry point without observability coverage.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API declaration; Scope section (In/Out); AC lines 1–5

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
